item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image is by John Morris, under a CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 license, which is incompatible with CC BY-SA 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really liked this one. The little discrepancies that foreshadow the last addendum, the plot twist, all the details, I love it.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The change has been made; thank you for your advice!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A quick Google search suggests that while both are (relatively) common in the UK, shotguns are easier to get licenses for and so would probably be easier to find someone with one. So I'd go with the .410.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can agree with this change. Which do you think makes more sense in this context?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Recommend changing the extremely unusual and rare .22 shotgun for a ubiquitous .22 rifle or .410 shotgun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Given the rarity of the weapon, which would only be magnified in the gun-unfriendly UK, I think it would be much more sensible to swap it for, well, anything else.

It's my suspicion that the OP is from the UK and for this reason isn't particularly familiar with firearms. It should be a .22 rifle or .410 or 20 gauge shotgun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i like the scp itself, but theres a little too many foundation mistakes for my liking so im staying neutral

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very clever (almost a little too clever for me, but I got it in the end), and funny in places too. I hit upvote at the Wikipedia line, but I would have upvoted by the end anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, I didn't pick up on any of that. I, too, thought it was just a creepy tree that told you to commit crimes…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. I understood the implications of the acorns and Skinner being corrupted, but I did NOT pick up on the rest of that until I went back and re-read. That's some effective subtlety right there. +1 for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am curious as to what Foundation would do with p-zombies that are detected.

They are not, by definition, disloyal or dangerous (because a p-zombie, by definition, is behavioraly exactly the same as a "person", just without the weird self-awareness crapaloo). If a loyal researcher was replaced, via anomalous means, with a p-zombie clone, the PZ would remain loyal to the Foundation's cause (otherwise it won't satisfy the definition of a p-zombie)

So it would be interesting to speculate how would Foundation react.

Perhaps there are SCPs that can only be safely handled by non self-aware entities ? (I realize that it's a bit banal and overlaps with WH40k, but that's just what immediately comes to mind)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm:

+ show block
– hide block

The name, 'Unreliable Oracle', refers not to the tree but to the article, which presents an incomplete/incorrect view of the skip, which is then revealed in the addenda. This is because the article is authored/edited by the skip itself through its Dr Skinner-puppet.
- The skip doesn't make you do bad things for the hell of it; that's the cover story, which stresses that there is 'no compulsion' to obey it. Everything it does in the PWF case is directed towards trying to stop the road building which would destroy the tree. When it becomes clear it can't stop the construction with its PWF-puppets, it has them stage a failed bank robbery and get arrested so it can reveal itself to the Foundation and save itself that way. It dispatches the last free PWF member with a rucksack full of its acorns as a back-up plan, having created a cover story that he might be an undercover policeman in a fairly clumsy attempt to stop the Foundation chasing him.
- After taking over Skinner it eliminates Professor Reeds, who was looking into the PWF and the discrepancies in the journal. It then secures itself a food supply (D-Class) and manipulates the Secure Containment Procedures to have Foundation personnel (an unknown number of which it is also taking control of) disperse its acorns for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I'm missing something here. Mostly, it seems like the bulk of the story is in the addenda and not the article, making it a tree with a face what makes you hear voices and do bad things. No-voting unless there's an explanation I've missed (I've been away a bit, maybe I'm just rusty).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hope this was clear to most readers, but just in case:

+ show block
– hide block

Acorns grow into oak trees; these are the acorns of a sentient, possibly malevolent psychic tree that takes over people and uses them to spread its acorns (which you'll notice can't grow near 1421 due to its energy-draining effect). It's reasonable to presume that any of 1421's 'offspring' would have similar anomalous properties if planted and allowed to grow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
P-zombies are an interesting candidate for putting into an SCP article. Would like to see that. I particularly like the interview with one half of the conversation real and the other a lie, though I wish there was a better spelled-out reason for the Foundation not using a D-class to communicate with an entity known to be telepathic.

Some kind of enhanced interrogation was my guess after reading 1416-but I vaguely wondered if there was some kind of extremely painful time travel-ish process that changed a person back into their past self.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not entirely sure I get what you're trying to get across, but I got glimpses and they intrigued me. Cautious upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An angel gets his wings?
The 7th Seal is broken and darkness envelops all?
Gilbert Godfried sings the hits of Celine Dion?
A squirrel knows where to find lunch?

Given the lack of information, it could be anything. But they could just as easily be Keter as Euclid.

Actually, come to think of it, it is just as likely that they are perfectly harmless acorns and the tree-oracle (treacle?) is making Skinner nuts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cheers - it's been difficult getting the balance right tonally, especially since:

+ show block
– hide block

Most of the journal is 1421 pretending to be the PWF member, having taken over his mind.

This article was actually the origin of Procedure 552-Hepburn, but it took so long to publish I've since worked it in elsewhere - without giving too much away Skinner's name and the procedure name were chosen to suggest a third party that was also the inspiration for the skip. My original feeling was that the procedure was a drastic scorched-earth policy, to be implemented when a Sector has been compromised and it's unclear who you can trust. With SCP-1416 I went a slightly more toned-down route and presented it instead as the destructive psychological interrogation of an individual, aimed at establishing whether they actually are who they seem to be. I may do more with this procedure, perhaps exploring (as it's a relatively common theme on the site), exactly how the psycho-epistemological specialists in charge of the procedure go about detecting mind-control, changelings who believe themselves to be the original, and p-zombies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wishun - what happens when you plant an acorn?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't figure out just what the real effect is, but possibly that's the point?
And on what basis does #7 figure the acorns are Euclid?

+1 for making me curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. I think it would be quite stupid for the Foundation not to monitor Skinner after his first exposure to 1421-1, so the whole drama seems somewhat staged to me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That picture is pretty freaky. And I loved the little memos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the ambiguity as to what Procedure 552-Hepburn is, and just what it's going to do to Dr. Skinner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the implications at play here. Also, the acorns leave a good sense of mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn, I love that recovery log. It's thorough, it's interesting, it's not overblown, and the depiction of the hippy cultists is even amusing (I laugh out loud at the "…but Wikipedia says he lives in Hong Kong" line). Skinner's manipulations were also very interesting to read - I love the sense of backstage drama in this piece, especially when it's not really clear what exactly Skinner was working towards.

Also, it's nice to see Procedure 552-Hepburn turning up again (is it going to become a signature a la Jekeled's damoriates?).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While they've never been made famous, yes, .22 shotguns really exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice storytelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I initially was wary of this because the concept seemed meh, but after reading through this, the story and execution are excellent.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm around 90% sure that there is no such thing as a .22 cal shotgun. Perhaps you were thinking of a .410?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A couple of the addendums are a bit long, but I like this overall. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the SCP itself, but I don't like the story told in the addenda. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you don't know what's actually going on, then I guess this can seem a bit generic to some.
Still, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, the man in charge is already corrupted by this.

In other news, reading this, then channelsurfing… and catching Pocahontas, when she starts to talk with the tree-face… takes a new meaning. XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the subtlety at work with this guys effect, and what's really going on. I especially liked the background Foundation drama we got a glimpse of through the document. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really had to think about this before I voted. I love the core concept of an oracle that gives you bad advice, but then you got bogged down in the recovery log. However, it started to pick up again once you get to the internal memos…

Too many little qualms to upvote, liked it too much to downvote. No vote for now.
